entries.description: The draw elbow is positioned too high and often overly extended, causing the draw shoulder to rise and hindering proper back tension engagement. This creates an inefficient line of force. (estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to pull with arm muscles
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle (flexion) is greater than 100 degrees
Draw shoulder height is elevated relative to the bow shoulder
coaching_cues: Elbow in line with the arrow
entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent anchor, poor string clearance, and reduced power transfer, causing vertical and horizontal inconsistencies.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 105-150 degrees high draw elbow chicken wing elbow in line with arrow drop elbow feel back muscles inconsistent anchor poor string clearance reduced power vertical horizontal inconsistencies

entries.description: The draw elbow is positioned too low and often overly flexed, which can lead to insufficient back tension, a collapsed draw arm, and an inefficient line of force. (estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to force the shoulder down too much
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle (flexion) is less than 90 degrees
Draw shoulder may be too low or forward
coaching_cues: Elbow in line with the arrow
entries.score_impact: Results in a weak and inconsistent anchor, difficulty achieving full draw length, and poor arrow flight due to lack of power and stability.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 60-85 degrees low draw elbow lift elbow elbow in line with arrow engage back muscles weak inconsistent anchor poor arrow flight lack of power stability

entries.description: The draw elbow should be aligned with the arrow, forming a straight line of force from the elbow through the hand to the string, facilitating efficient back tension and stability. (estimated ranges)
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle (flexion) is consistently between 90-100 degrees
The elbow is spatially aligned with the arrow's path
coaching_cues: Elbow in line with the arrow
Maintain a strong line of force
Feel the connection to your back
entries.score_impact: Promotes consistent anchor, efficient power transfer, and improved accuracy by maximizing back tension and shot stability.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 90-100 degrees optimal draw elbow alignment elbow in line with arrow maintain strong line consistent anchor efficient power improved accuracy

entries.description: The draw shoulder is raised towards the ear during the draw and anchor, indicating tension in the neck and upper trapezius, which prevents proper engagement of the back muscles. (estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to pull with arm/neck muscles
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height is significantly higher than the bow shoulder
Draw_shoulder_rise metric shows upward movement from setup to anchor
entries.score_impact: Causes significant inconsistency in anchor point and string clearance, leading to erratic arrow grouping and potential injury.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_height_diff 20-50mm elevated draw shoulder shrugging shoulder down back relax neck use lats inconsistent anchor erratic grouping injury

entries.description: The draw shoulder rolls forward or drops excessively, losing the connection to the back muscles and compromising the skeletal alignment for stability and power. (estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to reach the anchor point
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height is significantly lower than the bow shoulder
Draw shoulder position is forward of the spine
entries.score_impact: Leads to a weak and inconsistent anchor, reduced power, and difficulty maintaining a stable aim, resulting in poor grouping and vertical inconsistencies.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_height_diff -50 -10mm collapsed draw shoulder squeeze shoulder blades open chest shoulder back down weak inconsistent anchor reduced power poor grouping vertical inconsistencies

entries.description: Archery power and stability primarily come from engaging the large back muscles (rhomboids, latissimus dorsi) to pull the string, rather than relying on smaller arm muscles. This creates a stable platform for the shot.
entries.score_impact: Essential for consistent shot execution, improved accuracy, and injury prevention by distributing load efficiently and creating a repeatable release.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine back tension principle pull with back squeeze shoulder blades feel lats engage consistent shot execution improved accuracy injury prevention

entries.description: Practice drawing the bow slowly with a resistance band or in front of a mirror, focusing on bringing the draw elbow directly in line with the arrow and maintaining the draw shoulder down and back throughout the movement.

drill.name: Resistance Band Draw
drill.description: Using a resistance band, slowly execute the draw cycle, pausing at full draw to check elbow and shoulder alignment in a mirror. Focus on feeling back muscle engagement.

entries.score_impact: Improves muscle memory for correct draw arm and shoulder position, leading to more consistent shot execution and better grouping over time.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 90-100 degrees draw arm alignment drill resistance band draw slow controlled elbow to arrow shoulder down back muscle memory consistent shot execution better grouping
